title: Heaps
description: Use PriorityQueue for min-heaps and max-heaps to solve top-K, merge-K, and streaming median problems.

sections.title: PriorityQueue as Min-Heap
sections.explanation: PriorityQueue<T> is a min-heap by default—`poll()` always returns the smallest element. O(log n) for add/poll, O(1) for peek. Import `java.util.PriorityQueue`. Use `offer(val)` to add, `poll()` to remove min, `peek()` to view min, `size()` for count. Elements must be Comparable or provide a Comparator.

exercise.prompt: Create a PriorityQueue (min-heap) and add the values 50, 20, 40, 10, 30. Print the minimum (peek). Then poll all elements one by one, printing each, to show they come out in sorted ascending order.

validation.message: Use PriorityQueue with offer(), peek(), and poll().

exercise.hint: Min-heap always returns the smallest. Poll in a loop while !isEmpty() to drain in ascending order.

sections.title: Max-Heap with Collections.reverseOrder()
sections.explanation: For a max-heap, pass `Collections.reverseOrder()` to the PriorityQueue constructor. Now `poll()` returns the largest element. Alternatively use a lambda: `new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> b - a)`. Max-heaps are used for finding the K smallest elements, streaming maximum, and scheduling problems.

exercise.prompt: Create a max-heap using PriorityQueue with Collections.reverseOrder(). Add 15, 45, 25, 35, 5. Print the maximum (peek), then poll and print the top 3 values.

validation.message: Use PriorityQueue with Collections.reverseOrder() for max-heap.

exercise.hint: new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder()) makes a max-heap. peek/poll return largest first.

sections.title: Top-K Elements Pattern
sections.explanation: To find K largest: use a min-heap of size K. Add elements; if heap size > K, poll (removes smallest, keeping K largest). After processing all elements, the heap contains the K largest. For K smallest, use a max-heap of size K. Time: O(n log K) which beats O(n log n) full sort when K << n.

exercise.prompt: Given int[] scores = {72, 95, 88, 61, 99, 84, 76, 93}, find the top 3 highest scores using a min-heap of size 3. Print the three scores in ascending order.

validation.message: Use a min-heap with offer/poll to maintain top K elements.

exercise.hint: For each score: offer to heap. If size > 3, poll (removes smallest). At end, poll all 3 to get them sorted.

sections.title: Custom Comparator Heaps
sections.explanation: PriorityQueue accepts a Comparator for custom ordering. Common: sort int[] pairs by second element, sort strings by length, sort objects by a field. Use `new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> expression)`. For complex objects, `Comparator.comparingInt()` is cleaner. Custom heaps are essential for merge-K-sorted, task schedulers, and Dijkstra's algorithm.

exercise.prompt: Create a PriorityQueue of String sorted by string length (shortest first). Add "elephant", "cat", "dog", "butterfly", "ant". Poll and print all elements to show they come out shortest-to-longest.

validation.message: Shortest first: cat(3), dog(3), ant(3), elephant(8), butterfly(9).
Use PriorityQueue with a lambda comparator based on string length.

exercise.hint: new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a.length() - b.length()) sorts by length. Same-length order may vary.
